volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Pot of Gold (Section 2.8.385 - Section 3.5.525)

sections.label: Section 2.8.385
sections.original: nunc tusculum emi hoc et coronas floreas :
sections.translation: Now I ’ ve bought a bit of frankincense , and these chaplets of flowers ; these shall be placed upon the hearth for our household God , that he may grant a propitious marriage to my daughter . But what do I —? Do I behold my house open ? There ’ s a noise , too , within ; is it that I ’ m robbed , wretch that I am ?

sections.label: Section 2.8.390
sections.original: Aulam maiorem , si pote , ex vicinia
sections.translation: Seek of the neighbours a bigger pot [*] if you can ; this one ’ s too little , it can ’ t hold it .

sections.label: Section 2.8.391b
sections.original: Ei mihi ,
sections.translation: Woe to me ! By my faith , I ’ m a dead man ; the gold ’ s being carried off — my pot ’ s being looked for . I am certainly murdered , unless I make haste to run with all haste in-doors here ! Apollo , prithee do assist and help me , whom thou hast already , before this , helped in such circumstances .

sections.label: Section 2.8.395
sections.original: confige ságittis fures thensaurarios ,
sections.translation: Pierce with thine arrows the plunderers of my treasures . But am I delaying to run , before I perish outright .

sections.label: Section 2.9.398
sections.original: Dromo , desquama piscis . tu , Machaerio ,
sections.translation: Dromo , do you scale the fish . Do you , Machaerio , have the conger and the lamprey boned .

sections.label: Section 2.9.400
sections.original: ego hinc artoptam ex proximo utendam peto
sections.translation: I ’ m going to ask the loan of a baking-pan of our neighbour Congrio . You , if you are wise , will have that capon more smoothly picked for me than is a plucked play-actor [*] . But what ’ s this clamour that ’ s arising here hard by ? By my faith , the cooks , I do believe , are at their usual pranks [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.9.405
sections.original: fugiam intro , ne quid turbae hic itidem fuat .
sections.translation: I ’ ll run in-doors , lest there may be any disturbance here for me as well .

sections.label: Section 3.1.406
sections.original: † Óptati vires populares , íncolae , accolae , ádvenae omnes ,
sections.translation: Beloved fellow-citizens , fellow-countrymen , inhabitants , neighbours , and all strangers , do make way for me to escape ! Make all the streets clear ! Never have I at any time , until this day , come to Bacchants [*] , in a Bacchanalian den , to cook ; so sadly have they mauled wretched me and my scullions with their sticks .

sections.label: Section 3.1.410
sections.original: totús doleo atque oppidó perii , ita me íste habuit senex gýmnasium ;
sections.translation: I ’ m aching all over , and am utterly done for ; that old fellow has so made a bruising school [*] of me ; and in such a fashion has he turned us all out of the house , myself and them , laden with sticks . Nowhere , in all the world , have I ever seen wood dealt out more plentifully . Alackaday ! by my faith , to my misery , I ’ m done for ; the Bacchanalian den is opening , here he comes . He ’ s following us . I know the thing I ’ ll do : that the master himself [*] has taught me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.415
sections.original: Redi . quó fugis nunc ? téne , tene .
sections.translation: Come back ! Where are you running to , now ? Hold you !

sections.label: Section 3.2.415b
sections.original: Quid , stólide , clamas ?
sections.translation: Why are you crying out , you stupid ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.416
sections.original: Quia ad trís viros iam ego déferam nomén tuom .
sections.translation: Because this instant I shall give your name to the Triumvirs [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.416b
sections.original: Quam ob rem ?
sections.translation: Why ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.417
sections.original: Quia cultrum habes .
sections.translation: Because you ’ ve got a knife .

sections.label: Section 3.2.417b
sections.original: Cocum decet .
sections.translation: ’ Tis the proper thing for a cook .

sections.label: Section 3.2.417c
sections.original: Quid
sections.translation: Why did you threaten me ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.418b
sections.original: Istud male factum arbitror , quia nón latus fódi .
sections.translation: I think that it was badly managed , that I didn ’ t pierce your side with it .

sections.label: Section 3.2.419
sections.original: Homo nullust te scelestior qui vivat hodie ,
sections.translation: There ’ s not a person that ’ s living this day a greater rascal than you ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.420
sections.original: neque quoi égo de industria ámplius male plús libens fáxim .
sections.translation: nor one to whom designedly I would with greater pleasure cause a mischief .

sections.label: Section 3.2.421
sections.original: Pol etsí taceas , palam id quidem est : res ipsa testist ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , though [*] you should hold your noise , really that ’ s quite clear ; the thing itself is its own witness . As it is , I ’ m made softer by far with your sticks than any ballet-dancer . But what right have you to touch us , you beggarman ? What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.423b
sections.original: Quae res ?
sections.translation: Do you even ask me ? Is it that I ’ ve done less than I ought to have done ? Only let me —

sections.label: Section 3.2.425
sections.original: Sine , at hércle cum magnó malo tuo , si hóc caput séntit .
sections.translation: Now , by my faith , at your great peril , if this head should feel it !

sections.label: Section 3.2.426
sections.original: Pol ego haud scio quid post fuat : tuom núnc caput séntit .
sections.translation: Troth , I don ’ t know what may happen [*] hereafter ; your head feels it just now ! But what business , pray , had you in my house , in my absence , unless I had ordered you ? I want to know that .

sections.label: Section 3.2.428b
sections.original: Tace érgo .
sections.translation: Hold your noise , then ; because we came to cook for the wedding .

sections.label: Section 3.2.429b
sections.original: Quid tu , malum , cúras ,
sections.translation: Why the plague do you trouble yourself

sections.label: Section 3.2.430
sections.original: utrum crudum an coctum ego edim , nisi tu mi es tutor ?
sections.translation: whether I eat meat raw or cooked , unless you are my tutor [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.431
sections.original: Volo scire , sínas an non sinas nos coquere hic cenam ?
sections.translation: I want to know if you will allow or not allow us to cook the dinner here ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.432
sections.original: Volo scire ego item , meae domi mean sálva futura ?
sections.translation: I , too , want to know whether my property will be safe in my house .

sections.label: Section 3.2.433
sections.original: Vtinam mea mihi modo auferam , quae ádtuli , salva :
sections.translation: I only wish to carry the things away safe that I brought here ! I don ’ t care for yours ; should I be coveting your things ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.434
sections.original: me haud paenitet , tua ne expetam .
sections.translation: I understand ; don ’ t teach me ; I know .

sections.label: Section 3.2.435
sections.original: Quid est quá prohibes nunc gratia nos coquere hic cenam ?
sections.translation: What is it , on account of which you now hinder us from cooking the dinner here ? What have we done ? What have we said to you otherwise than you could wish ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.437
sections.original: Etiam rogitas , sceleste homo , qui angulós in omnis
sections.translation: Do you even ask me , you rascally fellow ? You who ’ ve been making a thoroughfare of every corner of my house , and the places under lock and key ? If you had stopped by the fireside , where it was your business ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.440
sections.original: non fissile auferres caput : merito id tibi factum est .
sections.translation: you wouldn ’ t have had your head broken . It has been done for you deservedly ! Therefore that you may now know my determination ; if you come nearer to the door here , unless I order you , I ’ ll make you to be the most wretched of creatures . Do you now know my determination ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.444b
sections.original: Quo abis ? redi rursum .
sections.translation: Where are you going ? Come you back again !

sections.label: Section 3.2.445
sections.original: ita me bene amet Laverna , uti te iam , nisi reddi
sections.translation: So may Laverna [*] love me well , I ’ ll expose you at once with loud abuse here before the house , if you don ’ t order my utensils to be restored to me ! What shall I do now ? Verily , by my faith , I came here with unlucky auspices ; I was hired for a didrachm [*] ; I stand in more need now of a surgeon than of wages .

sections.label: Section 3.3.449
sections.original: Hoc quidem hercle , quoquo íbo , mecum erit , mecum feram ,
sections.translation: This , by my faith , wherever I shall go , really shall be with me , and with myself will I carry it ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.450
sections.original: neque isti id in tantis periclis umquam committam ut siet .
sections.translation: nor will I ever again entrust it to that place , for it to be in such great peril . ( Speaking to CONGRIO and his SCULLIONS .) Now , then , go you all of you in the house , cooks and music-girls ; introduce even , if you like , a whole company of hirelings [*] ; cook , bustle , and hurry now at once just as much as you please .

sections.label: Section 3.3.454
sections.original: Temperi , postquam implevisti fusti fissorum caput .
sections.translation: O dear , I ’ m a ruined man .

sections.label: Section 3.3.455
sections.original: Intro abite , opera huc conducta est vostra , non oratio .
sections.translation: Be off ! your labour was hired here , not your talk .

sections.label: Section 3.3.456
sections.original: Heus , senex , pro vapulando hercle égo abs te mercedem petam .
sections.translation: Harkye , old gentleman , for the beating , by my faith , I shall demand of you a recompense . I was hired a while ago to cook , and not to be basted .

sections.label: Section 3.3.458
sections.original: Lege agito mecum . molestus ne sis . i et cenam coque ,
sections.translation: Proceed against me at law ! Don ’ t be troublesome ! Either cook the dinner , or away with you from the house to downright perdition !

sections.label: Section 3.3.459b
sections.original: Abi tu modo .—
sections.translation: Go there yourself then . ( CONGRIO and the COOKS and MUSIC-GIRL go back into the house .)

sections.label: Section 3.4.460
sections.original: Illic hinc abiit . di immortales , facinus audax incipit
sections.translation: He ’ s gone . Immortal Gods ! A poor man , who begins to have dealings or business with an opulent one , commences upon a rash undertaking ! Thus , for instance , Megadorus who has pretended that , for the sake of honoring me , he sends these cooks hither , is plaguing unfortunate me in every way ; for this reason has he sent them , that they might purloin this ( putting his hand on the pot ) from unfortunate me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.465
sections.original: condigne etiam meus med intus gallus gallinacius ,
sections.translation: Just as I might expect , even my dunghill-cock in-doors , that was bought with the old woman ’ s savings [*] , had well nigh been the ruin of me ; where this was buried , he began to scratch there all round about with his claws . What need of more words ? So exasperated were my feelings , I took a stick , and knocked off the head of the cock — a thief caught in the act .

sections.label: Section 3.4.470
sections.original: credo edepol ego illi mercedem gallo pollicitos coquos ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I do believe that the cooks had promised a reward to the cock , if he should discover it ; I took the opportunity [*] out of their hands , however . What need of many words ? I had a regular battle [*] with the dunghill- cock . But see , my neighbour Megadorus is coming from the Forum . I can ’ t , then , venture to pass by him , but I must stop and speak to him .

sections.label: Section 3.5.475
sections.original: Narravi amicis multis consilium meum
sections.translation: I ’ ve communicated to many friends my design about this proposal ; they speak in high terms of the daughter of Euclio . They say that it was discreetly done , and with great prudence . But , in my opinion , indeed , if the other richer men were to do the same , so as to take home as their wives , without dower , the daughters of the poorer persons ,

sections.label: Section 3.5.481
sections.original: et multo fiat civitas concordior ,
sections.translation: both the state would become much more united , and we should meet with less ill feeling than we now meet with ; both , they , the wives , would stand in fear of punishment more than they do stand in fear of it , and we husbands should be at less expense than we now are .

sections.label: Section 3.5.485
sections.original: in maximam illuc populi partem est optimum ;
sections.translation: In the greater part of the people this is a most just way of thinking ; in the smaller portion there is an objection among the avaricious , whose avaricious minds and insatiate dispositions there is neither law nor magistrate to be able to put a check upon . But a person may say this ; How are these rich women with portions to marry , if this law is laid down for the poor ?

sections.label: Section 3.5.491
sections.original: quo lubeant , nubant , dum dos ne fiat comes .
sections.translation: Let them marry whom they please , so long as the dowry isn ’ t their companion . If this were so done , the women would acquire for themselves better manners for them to bring , in place of dowry , than they now bring . I ’ d make mules , which exceed horses in price ,

sections.label: Section 3.5.495
sections.original: sint viliores Gallicis cantheriis .
sections.translation: to become cheaper than Gallic geldings [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.5.496
sections.original: Ita me di amabunt ut ego hunc ausculto lubens .
sections.translation: So may the Gods favour me , I listen to him with delight ; very shrewdly has he discoursed on the side of economy .

sections.label: Section 3.5.498
sections.original: Nulla igitur dicat “ equidem dotem ad te adtuli
sections.translation: No wife should then be saying : Indeed , I brought you a marriage-portion far greater than was your own wealth ;

sections.label: Section 3.5.500
sections.original: enim míhi quidem aequomst purpuram atque aurum dari ,
sections.translation: why , it really is fair that purple and gold should be found for me , maid-servants , mules , muleteers , and lacqueys [*] , pages to carry [*] compliments , vehicles in which I may be carried .

sections.label: Section 3.5.503
sections.original: Vt matronarum hic facta pernovit probe .
sections.translation: How thoroughly he does understand the doings of the wives ! I wish he were made Prefect of the manners of the women .

sections.label: Section 3.5.505
sections.original: Nunc quoquo venias plus plaustrorum in aedibus
sections.translation: Now , go where you will , you may see more carriages [*] among the houses than in the country when you go to a farm-house . But this is even light , in comparison with when they ask for their allowance ; there stands the scourer [*] , the embroiderer , the goldsmith , the woollen-manufacturer , retail dealers in figured skirts [*] , dealers in women ’ s under-clothing [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.5.510
sections.original: flammarii , violarii , carinarii ;
sections.translation: dyers in flame-colour , dyers in violet , dyers in wax-colour [*] , or else sleeve-makers [*] , or perfumers [*] ; wholesale linendrapers , shoemakers , squatting cobblers , slipper-makers ; sandal makers stand there ; stainers in mallow colour stand there ;

sections.label: Section 3.5.515
sections.original: petunt fullones , sarcinatores petunt ;
sections.translation: hairdressers [*] make their demands , botchers their demands ; boddice-makers [*] stand there ; makers of kirtles [*] take their stand . Now you would think them got rid of ; these make way , others make their demands ; three hundred duns [*] are standing in your hall ; weavers , lace-makers [*] , cabinet-makers [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.5.520
sections.original: ducuntur , datur aes . iam absolutos censeas ,
sections.translation: are introduced ; the money ’ s paid them . You would think them got rid of by this ; when dyers in saffron colours come sneaking along ; or else there ’ s always some horrid plague or other which is demanding something .

sections.label: Section 3.5.523
sections.original: Compellarem ego illum , ni metuam ne desinat
sections.translation: I would accost him , if I didn ’ t fear that he would cease to descant upon the ways of women ; for the present I ’ ll leave him as he is .

sections.label: Section 3.5.525
sections.original: Vbi nugivendis res soluta est omnibus ,
sections.translation: When the money has been paid to all the nicknack mongers , for these saffron-coloured garments and stomachers , your wife ’ s expenses , then at the last comes the tax-gatherer [*] and asks for money . You go , your account is being made up with your banker [*] ; the tax-gatherer waits , half-starved , and thinks the money will be paid . When the account has been made up with the banker ,
